The visits and instructions to the particular meetings and societies that place themselves under its patronage;
The convocation of the congresses and general assemblies.

These attributions the members of the committee will distribute among themselves, according to the specialty of each, they being, if it be necessary, assisted by a certain number of auxiliaries or of simple employees.
V.
Accessory and complementary institutions of the central committee.
Many complementary institutions will be annexed to the central committee, as local dependencies, in the measure in which circumstances permit it, namely:
A library, where all the works that interest Spiritism are found gathered and that may be consulted on the premises, or lent for reading outside;

A museum, where are collected the first works of Spiritist art, the most notable mediumistic works, the portraits of the adherents to whom the cause owes much for the devotedness they have shown it, those of the men to whom Spiritism renders homage, although strangers to the Doctrine, as benefactors of Humanity, great missionary geniuses of progress, etc.;
A dispensary destined for free medical consultations and for the treatment of certain afflictions, under the direction of a diploma'd physician;
A relief and providence fund under practical conditions;
An asylum;
A society of adherents, that holds regular sessions.
Without entering into a premature examination in this respect, it is fitting to say a few words concerning two articles, with relation to which misunderstandings may arise.

The creation of a general relief fund is impracticable and would present serious inconveniences, as we have already demonstrated in a special article. (Review of July 1866, p. 193.) The committee must not, then, take a road that it would have to abandon at the end of a short time, nor undertake anything that it is not certain of being able to realize. It needs to be positive and not lull itself in chimerical illusions. That is the means of walking a long time and with security. For this, it behooves it always to remain within the limits of the possible.

The relief fund to be created cannot and must not be more than a local institution, of circumscribed action and whose prudent organization serves as a model for those of the same kind that the particular societies may come to create. It is by their multiplicity that they will render effective services, and not by the centralization of the means of action.

It will be fed: 1st, by the portions, destined to it, drawn from the income of the general fund of Spiritism; 2nd, by the special donations made to it. It will capitalize the sums it receives, in such a way as to constitute for itself an income. With that income it will render the temporary or lifelong relief and will fulfill the obligations of its mandate, stipulated in the regulation of its constitution.

The project of an asylum, in the complete acceptation of the term, cannot have execution right at the start, owing to the capital that such a foundation would require and, moreover, because it is necessary to give the administration time to establish itself and to act with regularity, before complicating its attributions with undertakings that may fail. It would be imprudence to attempt many things, before being certain of disposing of the means of execution. This is what will be easily understood, as soon as one thinks of all the details inherent in establishments of that kind. It is fitting, without doubt, to nourish good intentions, but, above all, it becomes needful to be able to realize them.
VI.
Scope of action of the central committee.

In the beginning, a center for the elaboration of Spiritist ideas formed of itself, without premeditated design, by the force of things, but without any official character. It was necessary, since, had it not existed, what would have been the point of linkage of the Spiritists disseminated through different countries? Not being able to communicate their ideas, their impressions, their observations to all the other particular centers, scattered in their turn and not rarely without consistency, they would remain isolated, by which the diffusion of the Doctrine would suffer. A point of concentration was, then, indispensable, from which everything should radiate. The development of Spiritist ideas, far from rendering that center useless, will make its necessity felt still better, because the necessity for the Spiritists to draw near and form a sheaf will be the greater, the more considerable their number. The constitution of Spiritism, regularizing the state of things, will have the effect of making it produce greater advantages and fill the gaps that it presents. The center that this organization will create will not be an individuality, but a focus of collective activity, acting in the general interest and in which all personal authority is effaced. But what will be the scope of the circle of activity of that center? Will it be destined to rule the world and to become the universal arbiter of truth? To nourish such a pretension would be to misunderstand the spirit of Spiritism which, for the very reason that it proclaims the principles of free examination and of liberty of conscience, repels the idea of setting itself up as an autocracy; as soon as it did so, it would have entered upon a fatal path.

Spiritism upholds principles that, because they are founded on the laws of Nature and not on metaphysical abstractions, tend to become, and one day certainly will be, those of the universality of men; all will accept them, because they will find in them palpable and demonstrated truths, as they accepted the theory of the movement of the Earth; but, to pretend that Spiritism comes to be, everywhere, organized in the same form; that the Spiritists of the whole world will subject themselves to a uniform regime, to one same manner of proceeding; that they will have to wait for the light to come to them from a fixed point, a point on which they must fix their eyes, would be a utopia as absurd as that of pretending that all the peoples of the Earth will one day form a single nation, governed by a single head, ruled by the same code of laws and submitted to the same usages. There are, it is true, general laws that may be common to all peoples, but that always, as to the minutiae of application and of form, will be appropriated to the customs, to the characters, to the climates of each. As much will occur with organized Spiritism. The Spiritists of the whole world will have common principles, that will bind them to the great family by the sacred bond of fraternity, but whose applications will vary according to the regions, without, for that reason, the fundamental unity being broken; without dissident sects forming to cast stones and hurl anathemas at one another, which would be absolutely anti-Spiritist. There may, then, be formed, and will inevitably be formed, general centers in different countries, linked only by the community of belief and by moral solidarity, without subordination of some to others, without that of France, for example, nourishing the pretension of imposing itself on the American Spiritists and vice versa. The comparison, of which we availed ourselves above, with the observatories is perfectly just. There are such at different points of the globe; all of them, whatever the nation to which they belong, are founded on general principles established by Astronomy, which, nevertheless, does not make them tributary one to another. Each regulates as it understands its respective labors. They exchange their observations and each makes use of the Science and the discoveries of the others. So it will happen with the general centers of Spiritism; they will be the observatories of the invisible world, that will exchange among themselves what they obtain of good and of applicable to the customs of the countries where they function, since the object they aim at is the good of Humanity and not the satisfaction of personal ambitions. Spiritism is a question of substance; to cling to the form would be a puerility unworthy of the grandeur of the subject. Hence it comes that the centers that find themselves penetrated by the true spirit of Spiritism must extend their hands to one another, fraternally, and unite to combat the common enemies: incredulity and fanaticism. VII.
The constitutive statutes.

The drafting of the constitutive statutes must precede all execution. If it be entrusted to an assembly, it is necessary that the conditions that those charged with the labor must fulfill be determined beforehand. The want of a prior base, the divergence of points of view, possibly the individual pretensions, not to speak of the intrigues of the adversaries, might produce dissensions. A labor of such great reach cannot be improvised; it demands long elaboration, knowledge of the real necessities of the Doctrine, a knowledge acquired by means of experience and of serious meditations. In order that there be unity of views, harmony and coordination of all the parts of the whole, it has to emanate from individual initiative, reserving the possibility of receiving later the sanction of the interested parties. At the start, however, a rule will be necessary, a course traced, a determined object. The rule being established, one walks with security, without gropings or hesitations. Nevertheless, as it is given to no one to possess universal light, nor to make perfect anything whatsoever; as a man may be mistaken about his own ideas, while others may see what he does not see; as the pretension of one who wished to impose himself by any title would be abusive, the statutes will be submitted to the revision of the congress that is to gather most nearly thereafter, which may make to them the rectifications that seem convenient.

But a constitution, however good it may be, could not be perpetual. What is good for a certain epoch may become deficient in a later epoch. Necessities vary with the epochs and with the development of ideas. If it be not wished that with time it fall into disuse, or come to be set aside by the progressive ideas, it will be necessary that it walk with those ideas. With philosophical doctrines and with particular societies there happens what happens in politics and in religion: to accompany or not the propulsive movement is a question of life or of death. In the case here in question, it would be a grave error to chain the future by means of a rule that declared itself inflexible. No less grave an error would it be to introduce too frequently, into the organic constitution, modifications that would end by depriving it of stability. It becomes needful to proceed with ponderation and circumspection. Only an experience of a certain duration can permit one to judge of the real usefulness of the modifications. Now, who can in such a case be judge? It will not be a single man, who generally sees things only from his own point of view; nor will it be the author of the primitive labor, because he may be too complacent in the appraisal of his own work. It will be the interested parties themselves, because they experience in a direct and permanent manner the effects of the institution and can perceive wherein it errs. The revision of the constitutive statutes will be made by the ordinary congresses, transformed for that effect into organic congresses, at determined epochs, and thus it will be continued indefinitely, in such a way as to keep them, without interruption, at the level of the necessities and of the progress of ideas, even a thousand years hence.

The epochs of revision being periodic and known beforehand, there will be no occasion for making appeals or special convocations. The revision will constitute not only a right, but also a duty of the congress of the indicated epoch; it will be inscribed, beforehand, in its order of the day, so that it will not be subordinate to the goodwill of anyone whatsoever, and no one will be able to arrogate to himself the right to decide, on the strength of his particular authority, whether the revision is or is not opportune. If, after the statutes have been read, the congress judges any modification unnecessary, it will declare them maintained in their entirety. The number of the members of the congresses being necessarily limited, in view of the material impossibility of gathering all the interested parties in them, in order that those who gather not be deprived of the lights of the absent, all of these may, whatever the place in the world where they find themselves, send to the central committee, in the interval between two organic congresses, their observations, which will be placed in the order of the day of the coming congress.

No appreciable movement of ideas takes shape in a period less than a quarter of a century. Every twenty-five years, then, the organic constitution of Spiritism will be submitted to revision. Without being too long, that lapse of time is sufficient to permit the new necessities to be appraised and to avoid causing disturbances by effect of too frequent modifications.

Nevertheless, since it is in the first years that the greatest labor of elaboration will take place; since the movement to be operated on that occasion may cause unforeseen necessities to arise, until the society has firmed its steps; and since it matters that the lessons of experience be turned to account, without great delay, the epochs of revision will be nearer together, but always determined beforehand, until the end of the present century. In the interval of the first thirty years, the constitution will have completed and rectified itself sufficiently to enjoy relative stability. Then it is that, without inconvenience, the periods of twenty-five years may begin. In this manner, the primitive individual labor, which had opened the way, will become the collective labor of all the interested parties, with the advantages inherent in those two modes, without their inconveniences. It will modify itself under the empire of the progressive ideas and of experience, but without shocks, without precipitations, because it will obey the principle established in the constitution itself.
VIII.
Of the program of beliefs.

The absolute condition of vitality for every meeting or association, whatever be its object, is homogeneity, that is, unity of views, of principles and of sentiments, the tendency toward one same determined end, in a word: the communion of ideas. Every time that some men congregate in the name of a vague idea, they never come to understand one another, because each apprehends that idea in a different manner. Every meeting formed of heterogeneous elements bears within itself the germs of its dissolution, because it is composed of divergent interests, material, or of self-love, tending toward diverse ends that collide with one another and very rarely show themselves disposed to make concessions to the common interest, or even to reason; that bear the opinion of the majority, if nothing else is possible to them, but that never frankly ally themselves. So it was always, until the advent of Spiritism. Formed gradually, like all the sciences, in consequence of successive observations, its acceptance has gained little by little greater amplitude. The qualifier of Spiritist, applied successively to all the degrees of belief, comports an infinity of shades, from that of the simple belief in the manifestations, up to the highest moral and philosophical deductions; from the one who, halting at the surface, sees in the manifestations nothing more than a pastime, up to the one who seeks the concordance of their principles with the universal laws and the application of those same principles to the general interests of Humanity; finally, from the one who sees in the manifestations nothing but a means of exploitation for his own profit, up to the one who draws from them elements for his own moral improvement. For someone to say of himself that he is a Spiritist, even a convinced Spiritist, does not, then, in any way indicate the measure of belief; that word expresses much, with relation to some, and very little, relatively to others. An assembly to which all who say they are Spiritists were convoked would present an amalgam of divergent opinions, that could not reciprocally assimilate themselves, and would come to realize nothing serious, not to speak of the interested parties who would provoke within it the discussions to which it gave occasion.

That want of precision, inevitable in the beginning and during the period of elaboration, has frequently caused lamentable misunderstandings, causing to be attributed to the Doctrine what was nothing but abuse or aberration. By the false application that is daily made of the qualifier of Spiritist, criticism, inquiring little into the substance of things and still less into the serious side of Spiritism, found in it matter for mockery. Let an individual be called a Spiritist, or let him pretend to do Spiritism as conjurers pretend to do physics, though he be a mountebank, and at once he is considered a representative of the Doctrine. A distinction, it is true, has been made between the good and the bad, the true and the false Spiritists, the Spiritists more or less enlightened, more or less convinced, the Spiritists of heart, etc. But those designations, always vague, reveal nothing authentic, nothing that characterizes them, when one does not know the individuals and has not yet had occasion to judge them by their works. One may, then, be deceived by appearances, whence it results that the qualification of Spiritist, comporting no more than a flawed application, does not constitute an absolute recommendation, and that uncertainty casts into the Spiritists a kind of distrust, which prevents a serious bond of confraternization from establishing itself among the adherents.

Today, when no doubt is any longer legitimate about the fundamental points of the Doctrine, nor about the duties that touch all serious adherents, the quality of Spiritist may have a defined character, which it previously lacked. It is possible to establish a formulary of profession of faith, and the adhesion, in writing, to that program will be an authentic testimony of the manner of considering Spiritism. That adhesion, proving the unity of the principles, will be, moreover, the bond that will unite the adherents into a great family, without distinction of nationalities, under the empire of one same faith, of a communion of thoughts, of ways of seeing and of aspirations. The belief in Spiritism will no longer be a simple acquiescence, often partial, to a vague idea, but a motivated adhesion, made with knowledge of the cause and proven by an official title, conferred upon the adherent. To avoid the inconveniences of the want of precision, as regards the qualifier of Spiritist, the signatories of the profession of faith will take the title of professed Spiritists. Resting upon a precise and defined base, that qualification gives place to no misunderstanding, permitting the adherents who profess the same principles and walk by the same path to recognize one another, without any other formality than the declaration of their quality and, if it be necessary, the presentation of their title.
A formulary of profession of faith, circumstantial and clearly expressed, will be the traced road; the title of professed Spiritist will be the word of linkage.
But, it will be asked, will that title constitute a sufficient guarantee against those of doubtful sincerity?

It is impossible to obtain an absolute guarantee against bad faith, since there are persons who treat with disdain the most solemn acts; let us agree, nevertheless, that that guarantee is worth more than any other that does not exist. Moreover, the one who, without scruples, passes himself off for what he is not — when the question is only of words that fly away — often recoils before a written affirmation, which leaves traces and which may be presented to him in case he draw away from the straight road. If, however, there be some who do not let themselves be detained by that consideration, their number would be minimal and they would have no influence. Moreover, that hypothesis will be foreseen in the statutes, which will consecrate to it a special provision. Such a measure will inevitably keep away from the serious meetings the persons who would not be there in their proper places. If it had the effect of keeping away some Spiritists of good faith, these would be among those who are not sufficiently masters of themselves to declare themselves such, or among the timorous, who fear to put themselves in evidence, or, again, among those who are never the first to pronounce themselves, in any circumstances, before seeing what course things take. With time, some will enlighten themselves more completely and the others will take courage. Neither the ones nor the others, however, will be able to be counted among the firm defenders of the cause. As for those whose absence would be truly to be deplored, their number will be small and will diminish continually. Nothing being perfect in this world, the best things have their inconveniences. If one were to reject everything that is not exempt from inconveniences, nothing would be admitted. In everything it becomes needful to counterbalance the advantages and disadvantages. Now, it is too evident that, here, the former overcome the latter.

That not all who qualify themselves as Spiritists will submit to the constitution is certain; for that very reason, it will exist only for those who accept it freely and voluntarily, since it will not nourish the pretension of imposing itself on anyone whatsoever.

Since Spiritism is not understood in the same form by all people, the constitution appeals to those who envisage it from its point of view, with the object of giving it support, when they find themselves isolated, and of strengthening the bonds of the great family by the unity of belief. But, faithful to the principle of liberty of conscience, which the Doctrine proclaims as a natural right, it will respect all sincere convictions and will not anathematize those who uphold ideas different from its own, nor will it fail to turn to account the lights that may shine outside its bosom. The essential thing is, therefore, to know those who follow the same track. But how to know it with exactness? It is materially impossible to attain it by means of individual interrogations, with the additional fact that no one can be invested with the right to scrutinize consciences. The only means, the simplest, the most legal, would be to establish a formulary of principles, summarizing the state of present knowledge that stands out from observation and that has to sanction it the general teaching of the Spirits, a teaching to which each is free to adhere or not. The written adhesion is a profession of faith, which dispenses with any other investigation, leaving to each entire liberty. Consequently, the constitution of Spiritism has as a necessary complement, in what concerns belief, a program of defined principles, without which it would be a work without reach and without future. This program, fruit of the experience acquired, will be the marker indicating the road. To traverse it with security, alongside the organic constitution, there becomes necessary a constitution of the faith, a creed, if it be preferred, that may be the point of reference of all the adherents.

Nevertheless, neither that program nor the organic constitution can or must chain the future, under penalty of succumbing, sooner or later, under the coercions of progress. Founded in accordance with the present state of knowledge, it has to modify and complete itself in the measure in which new observations demonstrate to it its deficiencies or its defects. The modifications, however, must not be introduced into it lightly, nor with precipitation. They are to be the work of the organic congresses which, to the periodic revision of the constitutive statutes, will add that of the formulary of principles. Marching constantly in harmony with progress, constitution and creed will subsist in the succession of the ages.
IX.
Ways and means.

It is to be lamented, without doubt, that we have to enter into considerations of a material order, in order to attain an object wholly spiritual. It behooves us, however, to observe that the very spirituality of the work is bound to the question of terrestrial Humanity and of its well-being; that it is no longer a matter only of the emission of some philosophical ideas, but of founding something positive and durable. To imagine that we are still in the times in which some apostles could set themselves on the road with a traveling staff, without troubling to know where they would lodge or what they would eat, would be to nourish an illusion that bitter deception would very quickly destroy. For someone to do anything serious, he has to submit to the necessities imposed by the customs of the epoch in which he lives, and those necessities are very diverse from those of the times of patriarchal life. The very interest of Spiritism demands, then, that the means of action be appraised, in order not to be forced to stop midway along the road. Let us appraise them, therefore, since we are in a century in which it is necessary to calculate everything. They are great in number, as is seen, the attributions of the central committee, so as to need a true administration. Each of its members having active and assiduous functions, if it were constituted only of men of goodwill, the labors would be impaired, since no one would have the right to censure the negligent. For regularity of the labors and normality of the dispatch of business, it becomes necessary to count on men of whose assiduity one can be certain and who do not consider their functions as simple acts of obliging others. The more independence they are masters of, by their personal resources, the less they will let themselves be held by any occupations; if they do not dispose of time, they will not be able to consecrate it to those functions. It matters, then, that they be remunerated, as well as the administrative personnel. The Doctrine will thereby gain in strength, in stability, in punctuality, at the same time as it will constitute a means of rendering services to persons who have need of it. An essential point, in the economy of every provident administration, is that its existence not depend on eventual products that may be wanting, but on certain, regular resources, in such a way that its march, come what may, be not embarrassed. It is urgent, then, that the persons who are called to lend it their concurrence not feel themselves disquieted by the future that awaits them. Now, experience demonstrates that one must consider essentially aleatory the resources that have for their base only the product of quotas or contributions, always optional, whatever be the commitments contracted, and always difficult of collection. To base permanent and regular expenses upon eventual resources would imply a want of foresight, that one would later have to deplore. Less grave are, without doubt, the consequences, when it is a matter of temporary foundations, destined to last as long as they can; here, however, it is a question of the future. The fate of an administration such as this cannot remain subordinate to the hazards of a commercial business; it needs to be, from its very inception, if not so flourishing, at least as stable as it will be a century hence. In such a case, the most ordinary prudence dictates that the resources be capitalized, in an inalienable form, in proportion as they are obtained, in order to constitute a perpetual income, sheltered from all eventualities. The administration regulating its expense by the income it draws, its existence cannot, in any case, find itself compromised, since it will always dispose of means to function. It may, in the beginning, organize itself on a lesser scale; the number of members of the committee may be limited provisionally to five or six, the personnel and the administrative expenses reduced to the minimum possible, without prejudice to the development of the resources. In preparing the way for that installation we have consecrated until now the fruit of our labors, as we said above. If our personal resources do not permit us to do more, we have, at least, the satisfaction of having laid the first stone.

Let us figure, then, that, in one manner or another, the central committee, at a given time, is in condition to function, which presupposes an income of 25 to 30,000 francs. Restricting, in the beginning, its expenses, the resources of every kind of which it disposes, in capital and eventual products, will constitute the General Fund of Spiritism, which will be the object of a rigorous bookkeeping. The obligatory expenses being regulated, the surplus of the income will go to increase the common capital. Proportionally, it is with the resources of that capital that the committee will provide for the diverse expenses useful to the development of the Doctrine, without its ever making of it personal application, nor a source of speculation for any of its members. Moreover, the employment of the funds and the accounting will be submitted to the verification of special commissioners, designated, for that effect, by the congresses or general assemblies. The committee will have for one of its first cares to occupy itself with the publications, as soon as it is possible, without waiting until it can do so with the help of the income. The funds destined for this will not be, in reality, more than an advance, since they will return to the fund, by virtue of the sale of the works, whose product will revert to the common capital. It is a matter of administration.
X.
Allan Kardec and the new constitution.

As a prelude to the new constitution of Spiritism, which he was elaborating, and the disclosure of his manner of seeing with reference to his personal position, the considerations that we proceed to reproduce, extracted from the exposition that, with regard to the Fund of Spiritism, he made to the Society of Paris, on May 5, 1865, have perfect fitness in this preamble.

“Much has been said of the proceeds that I drew from my works. Certainly, no serious person believes in my millions, in spite of the affirmation of those who said they knew from a good source that I maintained a princely train, four-horse carriages, and that in my house one walked over Aubusson carpets. (Review of June 1862, p. 179.) Besides this, notwithstanding what the author of a brochure that you know said, proving, by means of hyperbolic calculations, that my budget of receipts surpasses the civil list of the most powerful sovereign of Europe, since, in France alone, twenty million Spiritists are my tributaries (Review of July 1863, p. 175), there is a fact more authentic than his calculations, that is: that I have never asked anything of anyone, that no one ever gave me anything for myself personally; in a word: that I do not live at the expense of anyone whatsoever, since, of the sums that were voluntarily entrusted to me in the interest of Spiritism, no portion was diverted to my profit. n “My immense riches would proceed, then, from my Spiritist works. Although those works have attained unexpected success, whoever is a little initiated into the affairs of bookselling knows that it is not with philosophical books that millions are gained in five or six years, when on the sales one has no more than the author's rights, which amount to no more than a few centimes per copy. But, large or minimal, that profit being the fruit of my labor, no one has the right to meddle in the employment that I give to it.
“Commercially speaking, I am in the position of any man who gathers the fruit of his labor; I run the hazards of every writer who can as well be successful as suffer a failure.

“Whoever has seen our dwelling formerly and sees it today will be able to attest that nothing has changed in our manner of living, since I began to occupy myself with Spiritism; it is now absolutely as simple as it was formerly. It is, therefore, manifest that my profits, whatever they may have been, were not enough to procure for us the enjoyments of luxury. What follows from that?

“Drawing me out of obscurity, Spiritism cast me into a new course; in a short time, I found myself dragged along by a movement that I was far from foreseeing. When I conceived the idea of The Spirits' Book, it was my intention not to put myself in any way in evidence and to remain unknown; but, the limits that I had imagined being at once surpassed, that was not possible for me; I had to renounce my taste for isolation, under penalty of abdicating from the work undertaken and which grew from day to day; it was needful for me to yield to the impulse and to take its reins. In proportion as it developed, a vaster horizon unfolded before me and pushed back its limits. I understood then the immensity of my task and the importance of the labor that remained for me to do in order to complete it. The difficulties and the obstacles, far from terrifying me, redoubled my energies. I descried the aim envisaged and resolved to attain it, with the assistance of the good Spirits. I felt that I had no time to lose, and I lost none, neither in useless visits nor in sterile ceremonies. It was the work of my life. I gave it all my time, I sacrificed to it my repose, my health, because before me the future was written in irrefutable letters. “Without drawing away from my kind of life, that exceptional position nonetheless did not fail to create for me necessities that only my personal resources, very limited, did not permit me to provide for. It would be difficult for another to imagine the multiplicity of the expenses that that position entails and that, without it, I would have avoided.

“Well then! gentlemen, what procured me a supplement to my resources was the product of my works. I say it with satisfaction, it was with my own labor, with the fruit of my vigils, that I provided, in its greater part at least, for the material necessities of the installation of the Doctrine. I thus carried a large contribution to the Fund of Spiritism; those who help in the propagation of the works cannot, consequently, say that they work to enrich me, because the product of the sale of every book, of every subscription to the Review, redounds to the profit of the Doctrine and not of the individual. “But, to provide for the present was not everything; it mattered also to think of the future and to prepare a foundation that, after me, might aid the one who would replace me in the great task that he will have to discharge. That foundation, concerning which I must yet keep silence, is bound to a property that I possess, and it is in view of this that I apply, in improving it, a part of what I earn. As I am far from the millions with which they have gratified me, I very much doubt that, notwithstanding my economies, my resources will ever permit me to give to that foundation the complement that I would have wished it to have, even in my lifetime. Once, however, that its realization is in the designs of my spiritual guides, if I myself do not do it, it is probable that, one day or another, it will be done. While I wait, I go on elaborating the plans that it is to obey. “Far from me, gentlemen, the idea of taking vanity, even slightly, in what I have just set forth to you. The pertinacity of certain diatribes was necessary, for me to decide, although against my will, to break the silence about some facts that concern me. Later, all those that malice has been pleased to distort will be made evident, by means of authentic documents. The opportunity for those explanations has not yet come. The only thing that for now mattered to me was that you should be enlightened with relation to the destination of the funds that Providence causes to pass through my hands, whatever be their provenance. I consider myself no more than a depositary, even of what I earn; therefore, with all the more reason, of that which is entrusted to me. “Someone asked me one day, without curiosity, be it well understood, out of mere interest in the thing itself, what I would do with a million francs, if I had it. I answered him that, at present, the employment of that sum would have to be totally different from what it would have been at the beginning. Formerly, with it I would have made the propaganda, by means of broad publicity; now, I recognize that this would be useless, since our adversaries have taken charge of defraying it. Great resources not then being placed at my disposal, the Spirits wished to prove that Spiritism owed its triumphs to its own force. “Today, the horizon being enlarged as it is and when, above all, the future has unfolded, the necessities that make themselves felt are of a very different order. A capital, such as the one figured, would have a more useful employment. Without entering into details that would be premature, I will say only that one part would be destined to convert my property into a special house of Spiritist retreat, whose inhabitants would gather the benefits of our moral doctrine; another to constitute an inalienable income, destined: 1st to maintain the establishment; 2nd to assure an independent existence to the one who would succeed me and to those who would aid him in the discharge of his mission; 3rd to attend to the current necessities of Spiritism, without the risks of eventual aids, as I am obliged to do, since the greater part of its resources flow from my labor, which will have an end. “There is what I would do; but, if such satisfaction be not given me, I know that, in one manner or another, the Spirits who direct the movement will provide for all the necessities in due time. For this reason, I in no way disquiet myself and occupy myself only with what, for me, is the essential thing: the finishing of the labors that remain for me to terminate.”
— To what he then said, Allan Kardec recently added:
When the committee is organized, we will be part of it as a simple member of it, giving it our collaboration, without claiming, for ourselves, either supremacy, or title, or any privilege.

Although an active member of the committee, we will in no way weigh upon its budget, neither by honoraria, nor by travel expenses, nor by any other cause. If we have never asked anything of anyone for ourselves, still less would we do so in this circumstance. Our time, our life, all our physical and intellectual strength belong to the Doctrine. We declare, then, formally, that no portion of the resources of which the committee disposes will be diverted to our profit.
We will give it, on the contrary, our contribution: 1st by relinquishing, in its favor, what our works, made and to be made, produce; 2nd by donating to it movable and immovable property.

Spiritism finding itself organized by the constitution of the central committee, our works will become the property of Spiritism, in the person of that same committee, which will manage them and care for their publication, by the means most appropriate to popularizing them. It must also care that they be translated into the principal foreign languages.

The Review has been, until now, and could not but be, a personal work, since it formed part of our doctrinal works, constituting the annals of Spiritism. It is through its intermediary that all the new principles were elaborated and delivered to study. It was, then, necessary that it conserve its individual character, in order that unity be established.

We were, on various occasions, solicited to make it circulate more frequently; however much that desire flattered us, however, we could not attend to it, first, because the material time did not allow us that increase of labor and, second, because it mattered that it not lose its essential character, which is not that of a newspaper properly speaking.

Today, that our personal work draws near to its term, the necessities are no longer the same; the Review will become, like our other works, made and to be made, the collective property of the committee, which will take its direction, for the greater advantage of Spiritism, without, for that reason, our renouncing to lend it our collaboration.

To complete the doctrinal work, there remain for us to publish various labors, that form not the least difficult, nor the least painful part. Although we already dispose of all the elements to execute them and the program of each is traced down to the last chapter, we could devote to them more careful attention and hasten them, if, the central committee being instituted, we were free of other cares that absorb a great part of our time.

— The first period of Spiritism was consecrated to the study of the principles and of the laws, that in their whole had to constitute the Doctrine; in a word: to preparing the materials, at the same time as to the popularization of the idea. It was that of the planting of the seed which, similar to that of the parable of the Gospel, would not fructify equally everywhere. The child grew; it became an adult, and the moment has come in which, supported by sincere and devoted adherents, it has to advance toward the object that is set before it, without being obstructed by the laggards. But how to make that selection? Who would dare to assume the responsibility of a judgment bearing upon individual consciences? The best would be that the selection make itself, and the means was quite simple: it sufficed to unfurl a standard and to say — let those who adopt it follow it.

Taking the initiative of the constitution of Spiritism, we have used a common right, that which every man has of completing, as he understands it, the work that he has begun, and of being judge of the opportuneness. From the instant in which each is free to adhere or not to that work, no one can complain of suffering an arbitrary pressure. We created the word Spiritism, to attend to the necessities of the cause; we have, then, the right to determine its applications and to define the qualities and the beliefs of the true Spiritist. (Spiritist Review, of April 1866, p. 111.) After all that has been said, it will be easily understood how impossible and premature it would have been to establish that constitution right at the beginning. If the Spiritist Doctrine had formed as a whole, like every personal conception, it would have been completed from the first day and, then, nothing simpler than to constitute it. But, having arisen gradually, in consequence of successive acquisitions, its constitution would have congregated all the lovers of novelties; soon, however, it would have been abandoned by those who did not accept all its consequences. Nevertheless, some perhaps will say: are you not thus provoking a split among the adherents? Opening two camps, do you not weaken the phalanx?

Not all who say they are Spiritists think in the same manner on every point; the division exists, in fact, and is much more prejudicial, because it may happen that one does not know whether, in a Spiritist, there is an ally or an antagonist. What makes strength is universality: now, a frank union could not exist among persons interested, morally or materially, in not following the same road and who do not aim at the same end. Ten men united by a common thought are stronger than a hundred who do not understand one another. In such a case, the medley of divergent views takes away the force of cohesion among those who would wish to walk together, exactly like a liquid that, infiltrating into a body, raises an obstacle to the aggregation of the molecules of that body. If the constitution has the effect of momentarily diminishing the apparent number of the Spiritists, it will have, on the other hand, as a consequence, to give more strength to those who walk by common accord toward the realization of the great humanitarian object that Spiritism is to attain. They will know one another and will extend their hands to one another, from one extreme of the world to the other.

It will have, moreover, the effect of opposing a barrier to the ambitions that, if they imposed themselves, would attempt to divert it for their own profit. Everything is calculated, with a view to that result, by the suppression of all autocracy or personal supremacy.
[1] See, for greater developments on the question of Spiritist institutions, the Spiritist Review of July 1866, p. 193.
[2] Those sums amounted at that time to the total of 14,100 francs, whose employment, exclusively in favor of the Doctrine, finds itself justified by the accounts.

chapters.title: SPIRITIST CREED.
paras: The ills of Humanity proceed from the imperfection of men; it is through their vices that they harm one another. So long as they remain vicious, they will be unhappy, because the conflict of interests will engender constant miseries.

Without doubt, good laws contribute to improving the social state, but they are powerless to render Humanity happy, because they do no more than restrain evil passions without eliminating them. In the second place, because they are more repressive than moralizing and repress only the most conspicuous evil acts without destroying their causes. Moreover, the goodness of the laws bears a relation to the goodness of men; so long as the latter remain dominated by pride and selfishness, they will make laws for the benefit of their personal ambitions. Civil law modifies only the surface; only the moral law can penetrate the innermost forum of the conscience and reform it. It being recognized, then, that the friction arising from the contact of vices is what renders men unfortunate, the only remedy for their ills lies in their improving themselves morally. Since the cause of the ills is found in the imperfections, happiness will increase in proportion as the imperfections diminish.

However good a social institution may be, if men are evil, they will falsify it and disfigure its spirit so as to exploit it for their own profit. When men are good, they will organize good institutions, which will be durable, because all will have an interest in preserving them.

The social question, then, does not have for its point of departure the form of this or that institution; it lies entirely in the moral improvement of individuals and of the masses. There is found the principle, the true key to the happiness of the human race, because then men will no longer contemplate harming one another. It is not enough to coat the corruption with varnish; it is indispensable to extirpate the corruption.

The principle of improvement lies in the nature of beliefs, because these constitute the motive of actions and modify the sentiments. It also lies in the ideas inculcated from childhood and which become identified with the Spirit; it lies further in the ideas that the subsequent development of intelligence and reason can strengthen, never destroy. It is through education, more than through instruction, that Humanity will be transformed.

The man who earnestly strives to improve himself secures happiness for himself, already in this life. Beyond the satisfaction he affords his conscience, he exempts himself from the material and moral miseries that are the inevitable consequence of his imperfections. He will have calm, because vicissitudes will only lightly graze him. He will enjoy health, because he will not ruin his body with excesses. He will be rich, because rich is always anyone who knows how to content himself with what is necessary. He will have peace of mind, because he will not experience fictitious needs, nor will he be tormented by the thirst for honors and the superfluous, by the fever of ambition, of envy, and of jealousy. Indulgent toward the imperfections of others, these will cause him less suffering and will rather inspire in him pity and not anger. Avoiding everything that might harm his neighbor, in words and in deeds, seeking instead to do everything that may be useful and agreeable to others, no one will suffer from his contact. He guarantees his happiness in the future life, because, the more he purifies himself, the more he will rise in the hierarchy of intelligent beings and the sooner he will abandon this land of trials for higher worlds, inasmuch as the evil he has repaired in this life he will not have to repair in other existences; inasmuch as, in erraticity, he will encounter only friendly and sympathetic beings and will not be tormented by the incessant sight of those who have grounds for complaint against him.

Let a few men live together, animated by these sentiments, and they will be as happy as our earth allows. Let a whole people, a whole race, all of Humanity gain these sentiments thus, step by step, and our globe will take its place among the happy worlds.
Is this a utopia, a chimera? It will be so for the one who does not believe in the progress of the soul; it will not be so for the one who believes in its indefinite perfectibility.

General progress is the resultant of all individual progress; but individual progress does not consist solely in the development of intelligence, in the acquisition of some knowledge. In that there is no more than a part of progress, which does not necessarily lead to good, since there are men who make ill use of their learning. Progress consists, above all, in moral improvement, in the purification of the Spirit, in the extirpation of the evil germs that exist within us. That is true progress, the only one that can guarantee happiness to the human race, since it is the very opposite of evil. The man of the most cultivated intelligence can do much evil; he who has advanced morally will do only good. It is, then, in the interest of all that Humanity should progress morally. But what do the improvement and happiness of future generations matter to the one who believes that everything ends with life? What interest has he in perfecting himself, in restraining himself, in taming his lower passions, in depriving himself of anything whatsoever for the benefit of another? None. Logic itself tells him that his interest lies in enjoying quickly and by every means possible, since tomorrow, perhaps, he will be nothing more.

The doctrine of annihilationism is the paralysis of human progress, because it circumscribes man's views to the imperceptible point of the present existence; because it restricts his ideas and forcibly concentrates them on material life. With this doctrine, man being nothing either before or after, all social relations ceasing with life, solidarity is an empty word, fraternity a theory without foundation, abnegation in favor of another mere deceit, selfishness, with its maxim—each one for himself—a natural right; vengeance, an act of reason; happiness, the privilege of the stronger and of the most cunning; suicide, the logical end of the one who, bereft of resources and expedients, expects nothing more and cannot extricate himself from the mire. A society founded upon annihilationism would carry within itself the germ of its impending dissolution. Different, however, are the sentiments of the one who has faith in the future; who knows that nothing of what he has acquired in learning and in morality will be lost to him; that today's labor will bear its fruits tomorrow; that he himself will be part of the generations to come, more advanced and more happy. He knows that, working for others, he works for himself. His vision does not stop at the Earth, it embraces the infinity of worlds that will one day serve him as a dwelling; he glimpses the glorious place that will fall to him, as to all beings who attain perfection. With faith in the future life, the circle of his ideas expands; the future belongs to him; personal progress has an aim, a real utility. From the continuity of relations among men is born solidarity; fraternity is founded upon a law of Nature and upon the interest of all.

Belief in the future life is, then, an element of progress, because it stimulates the Spirit; it alone can give man courage in his trials, because it furnishes him the reason for being of those trials, perseverance in the struggle against evil, because it assigns him a goal. To form this belief in the spirit of the masses is, therefore, that to which those who possess it ought to apply themselves.

Nevertheless, it is innate in man. All religions proclaim it. Why, then, has it not, to this day, given the results that ought to have been expected? It is that, in general, they present it under conditions that reason cannot accept. As they depict it, it breaks all relations with the present; once it has left the Earth, the creature becomes a stranger to Humanity: no solidarity exists between the dead and the living; progress is purely individual; each one, working for the future, works solely for himself, thinks only of himself, and that even for a vague purpose, which has nothing definite, nothing positive, upon which thought may firmly rest with security; in short, because it is more a hope than a material certainty. Thence results, for some, indifference, for others, a mystical exaltation which, isolating man from the Earth, is essentially prejudicial to the real progress of Humanity, inasmuch as it neglects the cares demanded by material progress, to which Nature imposes upon him the duty of contributing. Nevertheless, however incomplete the results may be, they are no less effective. How many men have not felt encouraged and sustained in the path of good by this vague hope! How many have not halted on the downward slope of evil, through the fear of compromising their future! How many noble virtues has this belief not developed! Let us not disdain the beliefs of the past, however imperfect they may be, when they lead to good: they were in correspondence with the degree of advancement of Humanity.

But, having progressed, Humanity demands beliefs in harmony with the new ideas. If the elements of faith remain stationary and become distanced by the spirit, they lose all influence; and the good they may have produced, at a certain time, cannot continue, because those elements are no longer equal to the circumstances.

In order for the doctrine of the future life henceforth to yield the fruits that ought to be expected, it is necessary, above all, that it completely satisfy reason; that it correspond to the idea one forms of the wisdom, justice, and goodness of God; that it cannot in any way be contradicted by Science. It is necessary that the future life leave in the spirit neither doubt nor uncertainty; that it be as positive as the present life, of which it is the continuation just as tomorrow is the continuation of the previous day. It is necessary that it be seen, understood, and, so to speak, touched with the finger. It is necessary, in short, that the solidarity between the past, the present, and the future, through the various existences, be evident. Such is the idea of the future life that Spiritism presents. What gives this idea its force is that it is absolutely not a human conception with the merit only of being more rational, without, however, offering more certainty than the others. It is the result of studies made upon the testimonies offered by Spirits of different categories in their manifestations, which permitted the exploration of extracorporeal life in all its phases, from the uppermost end to the lowermost end of the scale of beings. The vicissitudes of the future life, consequently, no longer constitute a mere theory, or a more or less probable hypothesis: they derive from observations. It is the inhabitants of the invisible world who come, themselves, to describe their respective states, and there are situations that the most fertile imagination would not conceive, were they not made manifest before the eyes of the observer. Furnishing the material proof of the existence and immortality of the soul, initiating us into the mysteries of birth, of death, of the future life, of universal life, rendering palpable to us the inevitable consequences of good and evil, the Spiritist Doctrine, better than any other, brings into relief the necessity of individual improvement. Through it, man knows whence he comes, where he is going, why he is on the Earth; good has an aim, a practical utility. It does not limit itself to preparing man for the future, it also forms him for the present, for society. By improving themselves morally, men will prepare on the Earth the reign of peace and fraternity. The Spiritist Doctrine is thus the most powerful element of moralization, because it addresses itself simultaneously to the heart, to the intelligence, and to well-understood personal interest.

By its very essence, Spiritism participates in all the branches of physical, metaphysical, and moral knowledge. The questions it involves are innumerable, which, however, may be summarized in the following points which, considered as incontrovertible truths, form the program of Spiritist beliefs.
Fundamental principles of the Spiritist Doctrine, recognized as incontrovertible truths.
Allan Kardec.

The corporeal death of Allan Kardec interrupted the Posthumous Works of that eminent Spirit. This volume ends with a question mark, and many readers would wish to see it answered logically, as that learned professor in matters of Spiritism knew how to do. Without doubt, so it ought to be.

At the international Spiritist and spiritualist Congress of 1890, its members declared that, since 1869, persevering studies had revealed new things and that, according to the teaching advocated by Allan Kardec, some of the principles of Spiritism, upon which the master based his teaching, had to be revised and brought into accord with the progress of Science, in general, over the last 20 years.

This current of ideas, common to the members of that Congress, who came from all parts of the Earth, proved that a new volume needed to be elaborated, in order to conjoin the teaching of Allan Kardec with what the search for truth constantly affords us.

That will be the work of the Commission of propaganda. We count greatly on the good counsels of the brothers who at the Congress demonstrated their competence concerning the highest philosophical questions, to assist the Commission in this elaboration of a collective and incessantly progressive work. This volume will in its turn have to be revised, when a new Congress so decides.
“Science,” said Allan Kardec, “has for its purpose to constitute the true genesis, according to the laws of Nature.”
“The discoveries of Science, far from debasing Him, glorify God. They destroy only what men have built upon the false ideas they have formed of God.

“Spiritism, advancing with progress, will never be surpassed, because, if new discoveries demonstrate to it that it is in error concerning a point, it will modify itself on that point; if a new truth reveals itself, it will accept it.” (Genesis, chapter I — Character of the Spiritist Revelation.)
P. G. Leymarie.

[1] [Although the title above indicates the existence of a program of Spiritist beliefs, this article published by Mr. Leymarie after the death of the Codifier is merely the preamble on the proposed theme and which was not concluded. The Codifier, however, did not leave us without the Spiritist Creed. — See also: Reasoned profession of Spiritist faith.]

chapters.title: A case of second sight.
paras: EDITOR'S NOTE. — Undertaking researches among old documents that belonged to the Spiritist Society founded by Allan Kardec, we found this page, alluding to a case of second sight, a page written, from beginning to end, by the hands of the master. We hasten, therefore, to reproduce it.
IMITATION OF THE GOSPEL.
PHENOMENON OF CLAIRVOYANCE.
Paris, October 20, 1863.

Miss V…, a native of Lyon, is endowed with a remarkable second sight, succeeding not only in seeing the Spirits in the normal state, without being placed in a somnambulistic trance, but also in observing, with great precision, the facts that unfold at a distance.

Once in Paris, where she came to spend a few days, she resolved to visit me, on Rue Sainte-Anne, having found my wife, since—after my return from Sainte-Adresse—I had withdrawn to Ségur, in order, with more tranquility, to work on my work about the Gospel. Our meeting was impossible, in view of the fact that the young lady had undertaken the return journey that very afternoon. But, during the conversation with my wife, the latter said to her: — “Since you will not be able to meet with my husband, which he will greatly regret, could you not transport yourself in Spirit to where he is, and see him?”
For an instant, the young lady withdrew into herself, and said:

— “Yes, I see him; he is in a very bright room, on the ground floor; there are three windows there… Oh!… and how cheerful everything is! The house is surrounded by gardens… everywhere trees and flowers… Everything breathes calm and tranquility… He is seated, near a window, working… He is surrounded by a multitude of Spirits who maintain his good health… there are some who seem very elevated, and who inspire him; one of them especially seems to be superior to all the others, being an object of deference to them. Question. — Do you perchance perceive the nature of the work with which my husband occupies himself?
Answer. — One moment… I see a Spirit who holds a book of large proportions… he opens it and shows me what is written there… I read it: Gospel.

OBSERVATION — Indeed, I was working on my book about the Gospels, and whose title still constitutes a secret to all. Miss V… could not have known it. As for my wife, she was unaware whether, at that moment, I was occupying myself with that or with some other subject. Nothing, consequently, could influence the thought of the clairvoyant. The description of the nooks is, moreover, precise, it being noteworthy that she never saw those places. The room where I had installed myself is provided with exactly three windows, which is not common, and, on all sides, adjoins the gardens. My wife was unaware that I was in this room, which is the parlor. She might, with much greater probability, have supposed me to be in the study. All the circumstances concur in proving that, in reality, Miss V… was witnessing everything, not being the plaything of her own imagination. Such a fact constitutes, for me, a proof of the interest that the Spirits took in this work, as well as of the assistance that they grant to me and to my activities. Allan Kardec.

Following the transcription of the document in the Portuguese language, the Brazilian Spiritist Federation delivers to the Spiritists pages 661 and 662 of the “Revue Spirite” of 1904, containing, in full, the original French text of UN FAIT DE SECONDE VUE. (Page inédite d'Allan Kardec.)

[1] Note from the Directorate of the FEB — In the course of the research we have been carrying out concerning Allan Kardec, gathering data for a series of articles that we began to publish, in 1974, in the “Reformador,” we came upon the page, now transcribed, printed in the Revue Spirite of November 1, 1904, 47th year, until then unpublished. We suggest to our readers the examination, in connection with the subject with which the Codifier was occupying himself in Ségur — Imitation of the Gospel (later, as we know it today, “The Gospel According to Spiritism”). (Reformador magazine of 1975, pages 219 and 220.)
